questions.questionText: Insulin is secreted by:
questions.optionB: Beta cells of the pancreatic islets of Langerhans
questions.explanation: Pancreatic islet cells: β cells (60-70%) → insulin, α cells (20-25%) → glucagon, δ cells (5-10%) → somatostatin, PP cells → pancreatic polypeptide, ε cells → ghrelin. Insulin is the ONLY hormone that lowers blood glucose. Mechanism: binds tyrosine kinase receptor → GLUT4 translocation (muscle/fat), glycogen synthesis, lipogenesis, protein synthesis. Reference: Guyton — Insulin.

questions.questionText: The anterior pituitary hormones include all EXCEPT:
questions.optionA: GH, TSH, ACTH, LH, FSH, Prolactin
questions.explanation: Anterior pituitary hormones: GH, TSH, ACTH, LH, FSH, Prolactin (+ MSH, endorphins). ADH (vasopressin) and oxytocin are posterior pituitary hormones (synthesized in hypothalamus — supraoptic and paraventricular nuclei, transported along axons to posterior pituitary). Anterior pituitary hormones are regulated by hypothalamic releasing/inhibiting hormones via hypophyseal portal system. Reference: Guyton — Pituitary Hormones.

questions.questionText: T3 and T4 are synthesized in the thyroid gland and require which element as essential component?
questions.explanation: Iodine is essential for thyroid hormone synthesis. Steps: iodide trapping (NIS — sodium-iodide symporter), oxidation (TPO — thyroid peroxidase), organification (into tyrosine residues on thyroglobulin → MIT, DIT), coupling (DIT+DIT→T4, MIT+DIT→T3), storage as colloid, release. T4 (thyroxine) is main secretory product. T3 is more active (most T3 from peripheral conversion of T4 by deiodinase). Reference: Guyton — Thyroid.

questions.questionText: Growth hormone (GH) acts mainly through:
questions.optionB: IGF-1 (insulin-like growth factor 1, produced by liver)
questions.explanation: GH effects: direct (diabetogenic — anti-insulin: lipolysis, gluconeogenesis, decreases glucose uptake) and indirect (via IGF-1/somatomedin C from liver: linear growth, protein synthesis, cell proliferation). GH secretion: pulsatile, peaks during deep sleep. Stimulated by: GHRH, ghrelin, hypoglycemia, exercise, amino acids. Inhibited by: somatostatin, IGF-1, hyperglycemia. Excess: gigantism (before puberty), acromegaly (after). Deficiency: dwarfism. Reference: Guyton — GH.

questions.questionText: Cortisol is secreted by the:
questions.explanation: Adrenal cortex layers (GFR = Salt, Sugar, Sex): Zona Glomerulosa → mineralocorticoids (aldosterone), Zona Fasciculata → glucocorticoids (cortisol), Zona Reticularis → androgens (DHEA, androstenedione). Adrenal medulla → catecholamines (epinephrine 80%, norepinephrine 20%). Cortisol: regulated by ACTH (CRH-ACTH axis), diurnal rhythm (peaks 6-8 AM). Functions: gluconeogenesis, anti-inflammatory, immunosuppressive, permissive effect. Reference: Guyton — Adrenal Cortex.

questions.questionText: PTH (parathyroid hormone) increases blood calcium by:
questions.optionB: Increasing bone resorption, renal Ca2+ reabsorption, and activating vitamin D
questions.explanation: PTH raises serum Ca2+: (1) Increases osteoclastic bone resorption (releases Ca2+ and PO4), (2) Increases renal Ca2+ reabsorption (DCT) and PO4 excretion (PCT — phosphaturic), (3) Stimulates 1α-hydroxylase in kidney → active vitamin D (1,25-dihydroxycholecalciferol) → increases intestinal Ca2+ absorption. Net effect: increased Ca2+, decreased PO4. Calcitonin (from thyroid C cells) opposes PTH → lowers Ca2+. Reference: Guyton — PTH.

questions.questionText: The most active form of vitamin D is:
questions.explanation: Vitamin D activation: skin (7-dehydrocholesterol → cholecalciferol D3 by UV-B) → liver (25-hydroxylase → 25(OH)D3, best indicator of vitamin D status) → kidney (1α-hydroxylase → 1,25(OH)2D3 = calcitriol, most active form). Calcitriol increases intestinal Ca2+ and PO4 absorption, bone mineralization. PTH stimulates 1α-hydroxylase. Deficiency: rickets (children), osteomalacia (adults). Reference: Guyton — Vitamin D.

questions.questionText: The Wolff-Chaikoff effect refers to:
questions.optionB: Transient inhibition of thyroid hormone synthesis by excess iodine
questions.explanation: Wolff-Chaikoff effect: acute excess iodine → temporary inhibition of organification and thyroid hormone synthesis (autoregulatory). Normally, the gland 'escapes' in 2-4 weeks and resumes normal synthesis. Clinical use: potassium iodide given before thyroid surgery (reduces vascularity and hormone release) and in thyroid storm. Jod-Basedow effect: opposite — iodine excess paradoxically causes hyperthyroidism in autonomously functioning thyroid (e.g., multinodular goiter). Reference: Guyton — Iodine Effects.

questions.questionText: Oxytocin is involved in:
questions.optionB: Uterine contraction during labor and milk ejection (let-down) reflex
questions.explanation: Oxytocin (from posterior pituitary): (1) Uterine contraction — positive feedback (Ferguson reflex: cervical stretch → oxytocin release → stronger contractions → more stretch → more oxytocin), (2) Milk ejection (let-down reflex): suckling stimulates oxytocin → myoepithelial cell contraction in breast → milk ejection. Also involved in social bonding, maternal behavior. Used clinically: labor induction (Pitocin), postpartum hemorrhage prevention. Reference: Guyton — Oxytocin.

questions.questionText: Prolactin secretion is tonically inhibited by:
questions.explanation: Prolactin is the ONLY anterior pituitary hormone under tonic INHIBITION (by dopamine/PIF from tuberoinfundibular pathway). Pituitary stalk transection → all other hormones decrease, but prolactin INCREASES. Prolactin-stimulating factors: TRH, estrogen, suckling, stress, sleep. Hyperprolactinemia causes: galactorrhea, amenorrhea, infertility (inhibits GnRH pulsatility). Treatment: dopamine agonists (bromocriptine, cabergoline). Prolactinoma: most common pituitary adenoma. Reference: Guyton — Prolactin.

questions.questionText: Addison's disease (primary adrenal insufficiency) presents with hyperpigmentation because:
questions.optionB: Increased ACTH (and MSH from same POMC precursor) due to loss of cortisol negative feedback
questions.explanation: In Addison's disease, adrenal destruction → cortisol deficiency → loss of negative feedback → CRH and ACTH markedly elevated. ACTH is cleaved from POMC (pro-opiomelanocortin) which also produces MSH (melanocyte-stimulating hormone). Elevated MSH → hyperpigmentation (especially in skin creases, buccal mucosa, scars). Other features: hypotension, hyperkalemia, hyponatremia, fatigue, weight loss. Addisonian crisis: acute adrenal failure. Reference: Guyton — Addison's Disease.

questions.questionText: The hormone that has the fastest mechanism of action is:
questions.explanation: Epinephrine (and other catecholamines) act via cell surface receptors (GPCRs → second messengers) → effects within seconds to minutes. Peptide hormones (insulin, GH) also act via surface receptors → minutes. Steroid hormones (cortisol, aldosterone, sex steroids) and thyroid hormones act via intracellular/nuclear receptors → alter gene transcription → hours to days for full effect. Exception: aldosterone has rapid non-genomic effects. Reference: Guyton — Hormone Mechanisms.

questions.questionText: The hypothalamic hormone that stimulates both TSH and prolactin is:
questions.explanation: TRH stimulates both TSH and prolactin from the anterior pituitary. In primary hypothyroidism: increased TRH → increased TSH (expected) + increased prolactin → can cause galactorrhea. This is why prolactin should be checked in hypothyroidism before attributing hyperprolactinemia to other causes. CRH → ACTH. GnRH → LH, FSH. GHRH → GH. Somatostatin inhibits GH and TSH. Reference: Guyton — Hypothalamic Hormones.

questions.questionText: Glucagon is secreted by alpha cells and primarily acts to:
questions.optionB: Increase blood glucose (glycogenolysis, gluconeogenesis in liver)
questions.explanation: Glucagon (counter-regulatory hormone): raises blood glucose by: (1) Hepatic glycogenolysis (glycogen → glucose), (2) Gluconeogenesis, (3) Lipolysis (releases fatty acids for fuel), (4) Ketogenesis in liver. Acts via Gs → cAMP → PKA pathway in hepatocytes. Stimulated by: hypoglycemia, amino acids, exercise. Inhibited by: hyperglycemia, insulin, somatostatin. Glucagon:insulin ratio determines metabolic state. Reference: Guyton — Glucagon.

questions.questionText: Cushing syndrome is caused by chronic excess of:
questions.explanation: Cushing syndrome: chronic cortisol excess. Causes: iatrogenic (most common — exogenous steroids), Cushing disease (ACTH-secreting pituitary adenoma, most common endogenous cause), ectopic ACTH (small cell lung carcinoma), adrenal adenoma/carcinoma. Features: central obesity, moon face, buffalo hump, stria, hypertension, hyperglycemia, osteoporosis, proximal muscle weakness, immunosuppression. Diagnosis: elevated 24-hour urine cortisol, overnight dexamethasone suppression test. Reference: Guyton — Cushing.

questions.questionText: Thyroid hormones affect metabolism by:
questions.optionB: Increasing BMR, O2 consumption, heat production (calorigenic effect)
questions.explanation: Thyroid hormones (T3/T4): increase BMR (~60-100% in hyperthyroidism), O2 consumption, heat production (calorigenic effect — all tissues except brain, spleen, testes), cardiac output (HR, contractility), protein synthesis/degradation, carbohydrate and lipid metabolism. Thyroid hormones are essential for brain development in fetus/neonate (cretinism if deficient). Act via nuclear receptors → gene transcription. Reference: Guyton — Thyroid Hormone Actions.

questions.questionText: The overnight dexamethasone suppression test is used to screen for:
questions.explanation: Overnight DST: 1 mg dexamethasone (potent synthetic glucocorticoid) at 11 PM → measure serum cortisol at 8 AM next morning. Normal: cortisol suppressed (<1.8 μg/dL). Cushing: cortisol NOT suppressed (ACTH not inhibited by normal negative feedback). High-dose DST differentiates pituitary (suppresses) from ectopic ACTH (usually doesn't suppress). 24-hour urinary free cortisol and late-night salivary cortisol are also screening tests. Reference: Guyton — Cushing Diagnosis.

questions.questionText: Pheochromocytoma is a catecholamine-secreting tumor of the:
questions.explanation: Pheochromocytoma: tumor of chromaffin cells in adrenal medulla (90%) or extra-adrenal paraganglia (10%). Secretes catecholamines (norepinephrine > epinephrine). 'Rule of 10s': 10% bilateral, 10% malignant, 10% extra-adrenal, 10% familial (MEN2A/2B, VHL, NF1). Paroxysmal hypertension, headache, sweating, palpitations. Diagnosis: 24-hour urine metanephrines/catecholamines, plasma fractionated metanephrines. Pre-op: α-blocker (phenoxybenzamine) before β-blocker. Reference: Guyton — Pheochromocytoma.

questions.questionText: Conn syndrome (primary hyperaldosteronism) is characterized by:
questions.explanation: Conn syndrome: aldosterone excess (usually adrenal adenoma). Aldosterone → Na+ retention → hypertension, K+ excretion → hypokalemia, H+ excretion → metabolic alkalosis. Plasma renin is LOW (suppressed by hypervolemia). Diagnosis: elevated aldosterone:renin ratio (ARR). No edema (aldosterone escape phenomenon). Muscle weakness (due to hypokalemia). Treatment: spironolactone (aldosterone antagonist) or surgery. Reference: Guyton — Conn Syndrome.

questions.questionText: The hormone that inhibits growth hormone release is:
questions.explanation: GH inhibition: (1) Somatostatin (from hypothalamus — also inhibits TSH, insulin, glucagon, GI hormones), (2) IGF-1 negative feedback (inhibits both GH and GHRH), (3) GH itself (short-loop feedback). GH stimulation: GHRH, ghrelin, sleep, exercise, hypoglycemia, amino acids (arginine). Hyperglycemia and free fatty acids inhibit GH. Octreotide (somatostatin analog) used to treat acromegaly. Reference: Guyton — GH Regulation.

questions.questionText: The Somogyi effect in diabetes is characterized by:
questions.optionA: Fasting hyperglycemia due to dawn phenomenon
questions.optionB: Rebound hyperglycemia following nocturnal hypoglycemia (counter-regulatory response)
questions.explanation: Somogyi effect: excessive insulin dose → nocturnal hypoglycemia → counter-regulatory hormones (glucagon, cortisol, GH, epinephrine) → rebound morning hyperglycemia. Management: REDUCE evening insulin dose. Dawn phenomenon: early morning hyperglycemia due to physiological GH/cortisol surge (no hypoglycemia). Management: INCREASE or adjust evening insulin. Differentiated by checking 3 AM blood glucose (low in Somogyi, normal in dawn phenomenon). Reference: Guyton — Somogyi Effect.

questions.questionText: Melatonin is secreted by the:
questions.explanation: Melatonin is produced by pinealocytes in the pineal gland. Secretion follows a circadian rhythm: peaks at night (darkness) and is low during the day (light inhibits via retino-hypothalamic-sympathetic pathway). Functions: regulates circadian rhythm (sleep-wake cycle), seasonal reproduction (in animals), antioxidant. Used clinically for jet lag and insomnia. Synthesized from serotonin → N-acetylserotonin → melatonin. Pineal tumors (germinomas) may cause precocious puberty. Reference: Guyton — Melatonin.

questions.questionText: SIADH (Syndrome of Inappropriate ADH secretion) causes:
questions.explanation: SIADH: excessive ADH → excessive water retention → dilutional hyponatremia, euvolemia/mild hypervolemia, concentrated urine (inappropriately high urine osmolality despite low serum osmolality). Causes: CNS disorders, lung diseases (especially SCLC — ectopic ADH), drugs (carbamazepine, SSRIs, cyclophosphamide). Treatment: fluid restriction, demeclocycline (blocks ADH action), vasopressin receptor antagonists (tolvaptan). Reference: Guyton — SIADH.

questions.questionText: Leptin is secreted by:
questions.explanation: Leptin: produced by adipocytes proportional to fat stores. Acts on hypothalamus (arcuate nucleus) to suppress appetite (satiety signal) and increase energy expenditure. Leptin deficiency (rare genetic) → morbid obesity. Most obese individuals have leptin RESISTANCE (high leptin but reduced response). Ghrelin ('hunger hormone' from stomach) opposes leptin → stimulates appetite. Leptin also regulates reproductive function (amenorrhea in anorexia). Reference: Guyton — Leptin.

questions.questionText: Sheehan syndrome is caused by:
questions.optionB: Postpartum pituitary necrosis (ischemic infarction due to hemorrhage)
questions.explanation: Sheehan syndrome: postpartum pituitary necrosis due to severe hemorrhage and hypotension during delivery. The pituitary gland enlarges during pregnancy (mainly lactotrophs for prolactin) → vulnerable to ischemia. Presentation: failure to lactate (first sign — loss of prolactin), amenorrhea, fatigue (loss of TSH, ACTH, GH). Posterior pituitary usually spared (different blood supply). Can be partial or complete. Reference: Guyton — Sheehan Syndrome.

questions.questionText: Insulin acts on target cells primarily by:
questions.optionB: Receptor tyrosine kinase → IRS → PI3K/Akt pathway → GLUT4 translocation
questions.explanation: Insulin receptor: receptor tyrosine kinase (RTK). Insulin binding → autophosphorylation → phosphorylates IRS (insulin receptor substrates) → activates PI3K → PIP2→PIP3 → Akt/PKB → GLUT4 translocation to membrane (glucose uptake in muscle and fat), glycogen synthase activation, lipogenesis, protein synthesis. Also activates Ras-MAPK pathway (cell growth). Insulin resistance: impaired signaling despite normal/elevated insulin. Reference: Guyton — Insulin Signaling.

questions.questionText: The calorigenic effect is NOT produced by thyroid hormones in the:
questions.explanation: Thyroid hormones increase O2 consumption and heat production (calorigenic effect) in virtually all tissues EXCEPT: brain, spleen, testes, and lungs. These organs have relatively constant metabolic rates regardless of thyroid status. The heart, liver, skeletal muscle, and kidneys are among the most responsive to thyroid hormone-mediated metabolic stimulation. This is a classic NEET PG question. Reference: Guyton — Thyroid Calorigenic Effect.
